title: This Movement Will Live Forever!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored; the printed 1970s historical reference, election statistics and community counts have been verified from the scans; and clear OCR character and word-break errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged and authoritative. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: The DMK's fourteenth general organisational election has proceeded systematically, stage by stage and on a democratic foundation. The newly elected General Council members will meet at 9 a.m. on Friday, 9 January 2015, in Kalaignar Arangam at Anna Arivalayam, Chennai. Elections for President, General Secretary, Treasurer and members of the Audit Committee will be held that day.

At the movement's thirteenth general election, there were thirty-five party districts. Following the resolution adopted by the General Council on 3 February 2012, the resolution of the High-Level Strategy Committee on 2 June 2014, and the recommendations of the resulting Administrative Reforms Committee, the organisation has now been divided into sixty-five districts.

Except in Salem and Erode, elections for district, city, union, town, area, town-panchayat, panchayat and circle organisations have been completed stage by stage in sixty of those districts. Elections to the headquarters organisation will now be held.

About 8.5 million DMK members participated in this fourteenth general election. Previously, union organisations operated directly above branch organisations. For easier administrative coordination between those levels, 12,514 new “panchayat organisations” were announced in this election. Elections have been completed for their 112,247 ward branches.

In the first stage, nominations for panchayat organisations were received in four phases, and elections in contested panchayats were held in five phases. The first took place on 26 and 27 January 2014; the second on 12 and 13 August; the third on 18 August; the fourth on 26 August; and the fifth on 5 September. Town-panchayat elections were likewise completed in five phases, and union and town elections in eight.

Some opposition members and a few journalists daydreamed that this election would create internal confusion in the DMK, that problems would erupt, that members would collide with one another, and that they could magnify the conflict and warm themselves by it. They wandered happily through that imaginary world. But their dream was shattered.

Some newspapers carried photographs of those who came to greet me for the English New Year. One appeared in *Malai Malar*. In it, younger brother Sekar Babu, unanimously elected secretary of Chennai East district, stood happily beside younger brother Renganathan, who had strongly opposed him and wished to contest for that district secretaryship. Only days earlier, both had competed intensely over who would become district secretary. During discussions, Renganathan willingly gave way. After Sekar Babu was selected, the two came together to greet me, and I was greatly pleased.

This must not remain an incident confined to one district. Across Tamil Nadu, those who contested and won, those who missed victory, and those who initially intended to contest but later yielded the opportunity must unite in feeling and work with determination to defeat our opponents in the 2016 Assembly election. That is the news I eagerly await. This is not a personal wish expressed for my sake. It is the right path for continuing to energise, strengthen and remarkably develop our movement.

All district-secretary elections were conducted over six days, from 25 to 30 December 2014, at the DMK office in Anna Arivalayam and at Rayapuram Arivagam. Headquarters representatives L. Balaraman, G. Selvaraj, A.T. Sadasivam and advocates R. Girirajan, K.S. Ravichandran and E. Paranthaman conducted them with twenty-five administrators from the Metropolitan Transport Corporation's Labour Progressive Federation.

Secretaries were selected unopposed in a few districts. Of the sixty district secretaries now selected, thirty-eight are new faces. Thus sixty-three per cent are newly selected. In only the remaining twenty-two districts were former Ministers or former district secretaries chosen. Among all sixty, only ten, or seventeen per cent, are former Ministers. Twelve had served as district secretaries during the previous term.

Udanpirappē, the country and the press have had the opportunity over recent months to learn of, report and understand our fourteenth general election because we have systematically conducted thirteen such elections over the past sixty-five years and are now holding the fourteenth. What limit can there be to the joy every DMK Udanpirappu feels because we sincerely cherish, protect and practise internal democracy?

In the 1970s, when Thanjavur was one undivided district, I wanted a member of an oppressed community to become a district secretary, and especially wanted that to happen in my own district. Everyone knows the effort I made to have younger brother Thazhai M. Karunanidhi elected unopposed as district secretary, and the success of that effort.

Our party constitution, revised from time to time, provides that at every level from panchayat organisation to headquarters, three deputy secretaries must be elected. One must be an Adi Dravidar or member of a hill community, one a woman and one from the general category. That amendment continues to be implemented. Thus every organisation under the DMK constitution necessarily includes representation for Adi Dravidars and women.

Two members of oppressed communities have become district secretaries in this election. Although I wished that at least three or four women would become district secretaries, only one woman has done so this time.

Our movement does not operate on the basis of caste or religion. Yet if we examine the communities to which the newly selected district secretaries belong, there are fourteen Mukkulathors, eight Kongu Vellalars, eight Vanniyars, six Naidus, four Muslims and three Mudaliars. There are two each from the Udayar, oppressed-class, Pillaimar, fishing and Reddy communities, and one each from the Chettiar, Yadava, Nadar, Christian Nadar, Gowda, barber and Mutharaiyar communities.

We have never selected people by asking which community they belong to. I give these true details only because some newspapers incorrectly claimed that this community received no place or that community no opportunity.

On behalf of headquarters, I offer heartfelt praise and congratulations to everyone who has assumed responsibility, after a contest, as secretary of a district, city, union, town, area, town-panchayat, panchayat branch, village branch or sub-branch, and to all who will assume office with or without a contest. I shall also have the opportunity to meet and congratulate you in person at the General Council on the ninth.

During the two years in which this fourteenth election has unfolded, thousands of dear Udanpirappus who contested organisational elections brought problems before us. The General Secretary, our esteemed Professor, Treasurer younger brother M.K. Stalin, Organisation Secretaries younger brothers T.K.S. Elangovan and R.S. Bharathi, headquarters managers Padmanabhan and Jayakumar, the staff and I had many opportunities to resolve them. It was an encouraging experience.

What were the disputes among those who came to us with requests? Competition over who would carry out the movement's work. Competition over who would stand foremost in bearing the ruling party's repression. Ours is a movement that grows every day, a movement that has matured, a movement that has created a magnificent history, and a movement that keeps growing. These were welcome competitions.

That is why I do not call those who prevailed “victors”; I call them those who were selected. Those appointed without a contest, those appointed after contesting, and those who contested but could not assume office are all alike to me. Just as parents regard every child without discrimination as their own, all the movement's Udanpirappus are Udanpirappus dearer to me than life. I gather you all into an embrace, welcome you wholeheartedly and offer my congratulations. This movement will live forever!

Our work does not end here. The General Council will meet in Chennai on the ninth. It will elect the President, General Secretary and Treasurer who will lead the movement for the next five years. You who obtained the votes of members at different levels and assumed responsibility as General Council members, Executive Committee members, and union, town and district secretaries will vote for these offices.
The General Council must also elect Audit Committee members to examine the movement's accounts for the next five years. It must frame resolutions on the work we must undertake next.

I therefore extend my affectionate invitation to all General Council members, Executive Committee members and district secretaries to attend and grace the meeting. With great happiness, I tell you that I shall meet you all there in person. Saying that I await that moment, I conclude this letter.
With affection,
M.K.
3 January 2015
